title: Monohybrid inheritance — dominant and recessive
examTip: Punnett squares appear in almost every AQA genetics paper. Practise drawing them quickly and accurately, and make sure you can convert between genotype and phenotype. The 3:1 ratio from Ff × Ff is a classic exam question.
introTips: This topic is where genetics stops being abstract and becomes something you can actually calculate. Once you understand the Punnett square, you can predict the probability of any genetic outcome.
The dominant/recessive rule feels counter-intuitive at first — two parents can both look unaffected but still pass on a genetic disease. Understanding carriers is the key insight.
Cystic fibrosis and polydactyly are the two AQA named conditions — know which is dominant and which is recessive, and you'll be ready for any question about inheritance of genetic disorders.
intro: Why do some traits skip generations? Why can two brown-eyed parents have a blue-eyed child? The answer lies in how alleles — different versions of genes — are inherited and expressed. Gregor Mendel worked this out in the 1860s using pea plants, and the same rules apply to every sexually reproducing organism, including you. This lesson gives you the framework that makes sense of family traits, genetic diseases, and probability in biology.

keyPoints.heading: Alleles, genotypes and phenotypes
keyPoints.content: A gene can have different versions called alleles. You inherit two alleles for each gene — one from each parent. A dominant allele is always expressed when present (written as a capital letter, e.g. F). A recessive allele is only expressed when two copies are present (written as lower-case, e.g. f). Your genotype is the combination of alleles you carry (e.g. Ff, FF, or ff). Your phenotype is the physical characteristic that results (e.g. 'has cystic fibrosis' or 'unaffected'). Organisms with two identical alleles are homozygous (FF or ff); those with two different alleles are heterozygous (Ff).
writeBullets: Allele = a version of a gene (same gene, different form)
Dominant allele (capital letter): expressed even with one copy
Recessive allele (lower case): only expressed with two copies (homozygous recessive)
Genotype = the alleles you carry (e.g. Ff, FF, ff)
Phenotype = the observable characteristic (e.g. affected / unaffected)
Homozygous = two same alleles (FF or ff); Heterozygous = two different alleles (Ff)

keyTerms.def: A version of a gene — e.g. the gene for flower colour may have a 'purple' allele and a 'white' allele
An allele that is expressed in the phenotype even when only one copy is present
An allele that is only expressed in the phenotype when two copies are present (homozygous recessive)
The combination of alleles an organism carries for a particular gene
The observable characteristic produced by the genotype
Having two identical alleles for a gene (e.g. FF or ff)
Having two different alleles for a gene (e.g. Ff)

keyPoints.memTip: Dominant = Decides (takes charge even alone). Recessive = Retiring (hides away unless it gets two copies). Genotype = the Genetic letters. Phenotype = what you actually see (Ph = Physical).

keyPoints.heading: Punnett squares and predicting inheritance
keyPoints.content: A Punnett square is a grid used to predict the possible genotypes of offspring from a cross. Write the alleles of one parent along the top and the other parent down the side, then fill in the boxes by combining one allele from each parent. For a cross between two carriers (Ff × Ff): the offspring genotypes are FF, Ff, Ff, and ff in a 1:2:1 ratio. Because FF and Ff both have the dominant phenotype, the phenotype ratio is 3:1 (three unaffected : one affected). Each offspring is an independent event — a 1-in-4 (25%) probability, not a guarantee. You can also calculate the probability of a specific genotype as a fraction or percentage.
writeBullets: Punnett square: parental alleles along top and side → fill boxes by combining one from each
Ff × Ff gives: FF, Ff, Ff, ff (1:2:1 genotype ratio)
FF and Ff both show dominant phenotype → 3:1 phenotype ratio
Each offspring is independent — 25% chance of ff (affected), not certainty
Express probability as a fraction (1/4), ratio (1:3), or percentage (25%)

keyTerms.def: A grid used to predict the genotype and phenotype ratios of offspring from a genetic cross
The phenotype ratio expected from crossing two heterozygous parents (Ff × Ff) — three with dominant phenotype, one with recessive phenotype
An organism that is heterozygous for a recessive condition (Ff) — does not show the condition but can pass the recessive allele to offspring

keyPoints.memTip: For Ff × Ff: think of a 2×2 grid. Top row: F and f. Left column: F and f. Fill in: FF, Ff, Ff, ff. Count the phenotypes: three show dominant (FF + Ff + Ff), one shows recessive (ff). 3:1. Commit that grid to muscle memory.

keyPoints.heading: Genetic disorders — cystic fibrosis and polydactyly
keyPoints.content: AQA requires you to know two specific genetic disorders. Cystic fibrosis is caused by a recessive allele (f). A person must inherit two copies (ff) to be affected. Parents who are carriers (Ff) appear unaffected but can pass the condition to children — this is why conditions can appear to skip generations. Polydactyly (extra fingers or toes) is caused by a dominant allele (D). A single copy (Dd) is enough to cause the condition, so it cannot skip generations — at least one parent must be affected or carry the allele. Carriers exist for recessive conditions only — there is no such thing as a 'carrier' of a dominant condition.
writeBullets: Cystic fibrosis: recessive (ff needed to be affected); carriers (Ff) are unaffected
CF can appear to skip generations — both parents Ff × Ff gives 1/4 chance of ff child
Polydactyly: dominant (Dd or DD) — just one copy causes the condition
Polydactyly cannot skip generations — affected parent always present
Carriers only exist for recessive conditions, not dominant ones

keyTerms.def: A recessive genetic disorder (ff) causing thick mucus in the lungs and digestive system
A dominant genetic disorder (Dd or DD) causing extra fingers or toes
A heterozygous individual (Ff) who carries a recessive allele but does not show the condition

keyPoints.memTip: CF = Carefully hidden (recessive, needs two copies, can skip generations). Polydactyly = Proudly present (dominant, always shows if you have the allele). One capital letter D is enough to give you extra digits.

tables.title: Cystic fibrosis inheritance — Ff × Ff cross

1.question: A student says: 'Because an allele is recessive, it will never be seen in the phenotype.' Is this correct?
options: Yes — recessive alleles are always hidden by dominant ones
No — a recessive allele is expressed when two copies are present (homozygous recessive)
No — recessive alleles are more common than dominant ones, so they are always expressed
Yes — only dominant alleles affect phenotype
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — a recessive allele is expressed when an organism is homozygous recessive (e.g. ff). It's only masked when paired with a dominant allele (Ff).
1.wrongFeedback: A recessive allele is expressed in the phenotype when two copies are present — the homozygous recessive genotype (e.g. ff). When paired with a dominant allele (Ff), the recessive allele is masked — but it's still there and can be passed to offspring.

2.question: Two carriers (Ff) for cystic fibrosis have children. What is the probability that a child has cystic fibrosis?
options: 0% — carriers cannot pass on the condition
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — from Ff × Ff, one quarter of offspring are ff (affected). That's a 25% (1 in 4) probability for each child.
2.wrongFeedback: Draw the Punnett square: Ff × Ff gives FF, Ff, Ff, ff. Only ff shows cystic fibrosis — that's 1 out of 4 combinations, so 25% probability. Remember this is per child — it's an independent event each time.

3.question: Why can cystic fibrosis appear to skip a generation, but polydactyly cannot?
options: Cystic fibrosis is caused by a virus that disappears between generations
Cystic fibrosis is recessive — unaffected carriers (Ff) can pass on the allele; polydactyly is dominant and always shows if the allele is present
Polydactyly only affects females, so it appears to skip male generations
Cystic fibrosis is caused by an X-linked gene, which skips generations in males
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly — carriers of cystic fibrosis (Ff) are phenotypically unaffected, so the condition can disappear from sight for a generation. Polydactyly is dominant, so you can never be a silent carrier — if you have the D allele, you show the condition.
3.wrongFeedback: The key is dominant vs recessive. Cystic fibrosis is recessive (ff) — carriers (Ff) show no symptoms but can pass the allele on silently. Polydactyly is dominant — a single D allele always shows in the phenotype, so at least one parent must be affected.

commonMistakes: Using the wrong case for alleles in Punnett squares — dominant is always upper case (F), recessive always lower case (f). Mixing cases confuses the examiner and loses marks.
Saying the 3:1 ratio is guaranteed — it's a probability ratio, not a certainty. For any individual child, each outcome is independent.
Calling heterozygous individuals 'affected' for recessive conditions — they are carriers but unaffected. Only homozygous recessive (ff) individuals show the condition.
Confusing which disorder is dominant and which is recessive — cystic fibrosis is recessive; polydactyly is dominant. A common exam trap.
Thinking carriers exist for dominant conditions — they don't. If you carry a dominant allele, you show the phenotype. Carriers are a concept that only applies to recessive conditions.
examTips: Always show your working for Punnett squares — draw the grid, label the parental alleles, fill in every box. Even if you get the final answer wrong, method marks are available.
When giving a probability, state it clearly — '25% probability' or '1 in 4 chance'. Avoid saying 'likely' or 'unlikely' without a number.
For questions about genetic disorders, always identify whether the allele is dominant or recessive first — this determines whether the condition can skip generations and whether carriers exist.

revisionCardBullets: Allele = a version of a gene — dominant (capital) or recessive (lower case)
Dominant: expressed with one or two copies. Recessive: only expressed with two copies (homozygous recessive)
Genotype = allele combination (FF, Ff, ff). Phenotype = observable characteristic
Homozygous = two same alleles (FF or ff). Heterozygous = two different (Ff)
Punnett square: put parental alleles along top and side, fill each box by combining one from each parent
Ff × Ff → offspring: FF, Ff, Ff, ff → phenotype ratio 3:1 (dominant : recessive)
Probability of ff from Ff × Ff = 25% (1 in 4) per child
Cystic fibrosis: recessive (ff) — carriers (Ff) unaffected, condition can skip generations
Polydactyly: dominant (D allele) — always expressed, cannot skip generations
Carriers only exist for recessive conditions — dominant alleles always show in phenotype
